Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Late Antiquity

Maximus the Confessor

c. 580 – 662

Intellect, will, and the soul’s ascent through deification.

Maximus the Confessor can yield a serious and surprisingly comprehensive philosophy of mind, but only if “mind” is widened beyond the modern problem of consciousness narrowly conceived. For Maximus, the mind is not merely an inner theater, a brain function, or a faculty of proposition-handling. It is the noetic, rational, affective, volitional, embodied, liturgical, and cosmic activity of a creature made to move toward God. His philosophy of mind is therefore inseparable from ontology, Christology, virtue theory, ascetic therapy, angelology, and apophatic theology. That does not make it less philosophical. It makes the philosophy of mind more architectonic.

The core Maximian picture is this: the human person is a composite of body and rational-intellectual soul; the soul is not reducible to the body, but neither is the human being reducible to the soul. The soul’s highest contemplative power is the noûs, often translated “intellect,” “mind,” or “spiritual intellect”; its active-discursive power is logos/reason; its sensory and passible powers connect it to body, world, desire, anger, pleasure, pain, memory, and imagination. Mental life is not morally neutral in the modern sense, because cognition is always situated within a movement of the whole person toward or away from its proper end. Yet Maximus is not anti-body, anti-affect, or anti-sense. His mental theory is therapeutic: wrong use of mental images, passions, and will deforms the mind; love, self-control, contemplation, prayer, and sacramental-liturgical participation heal and transfigure it.

Maximus’s thought about God and angels is not ornamental. It is central. God is not, for Maximus, a “mind” in the same genus as human minds; indeed, he explicitly denies that God can be conceived simply as intellect or intelligible being. But created intellect exists because of the divine Logos and the logoi, the intelligible principles of creatures. Angelic minds likewise matter because they show that intellective life is not exhausted by human embodiment, while human beings remain distinct precisely because they bind sensible and intelligible creation together. The Maximian philosophy of mind is therefore not merely “psychology plus spirituality.” It is a theory of created intelligibility, embodied cognition, affective intentionality, free self-determination, and deifying participation under the horizon of the unknowable God.

1. Methodological orientation

Maximus, who lived roughly from 580 to 662, is not a “philosopher of mind” by genre. He writes as monk, exegete, theologian, controversialist, and spiritual master. Still, modern scholarship has increasingly treated him as one of the most philosophically sophisticated late antique Christian thinkers. David Bradshaw’s Cambridge overview describes Maximus as presenting the philosophy of the Greek-speaking Christian tradition in one of its most fully developed forms, drawing on Alexandrian, Cappadocian, Evagrian, Dionysian, and Cyrilline sources while giving them a distinctive Christological and cosmic synthesis.

The right method, then, is neither to strip away theology in order to find a “pure” psychology, nor to dissolve every philosophical claim into pious language. Maximus’s claims about noûs, soul, reason, will, passions, logoi, angels, and God are mutually implicative. A “philosophy of mind” extracted from him must therefore include what modern philosophy often brackets: the end of mind, the moral quality of attention, the ontology of intelligibility, the body’s role in thought, the therapeutic role of virtue, the intellect’s capacity for divine participation, and the difference between created and uncreated knowing.

There is also a translation problem. Greek noûs is not simply “mind” in the modern English sense. It can mean intellect, spiritual perception, contemplative awareness, or the highest power of the soul. Logos can mean word, reason, rational principle, account, or inner structure; logoi are the creaturely principles or divine intentions according to which beings exist. Pathē are not simply “emotions”; they are passions or passible movements, which can be disordered or transformed. Gnōmē is not simply “choice”; it is opinion, inclination, intention, or the concrete personal mode of willing. These terms need to be allowed their Maximian range.

2. Core vocabulary for a Maximian philosophy of mind

Term Approximate meaning Role in philosophy of mind psychē soul The life-bearing, rational-intellectual principle of the human being; not the whole person by itself. noûs intellect, spiritual mind Highest contemplative power; capable of direct apprehension, prayer, divine illumination, and contemplation of logoi. logos / logoi reason, rational principle / inner principles Discursive reason in the soul; also the divine-rational principles of beings in the Logos. dianoia / logismos thinking, reasoning, thought Discursive and representational mental activity; often the field where passions attach to images. aisthēsis sense perception The soul’s contact with material reality through the body; natural and good, but vulnerable to misuse. pathē passions Movements or states of the passible soul; not evil as such, but disordered when contrary to nature. epithymia desire The soul’s desiring power; corrupted as lust/greed, transformed as longing for God. thymos incensive power Anger/spiritedness; corrupted as aggression or resentment, transformed into loving zeal. thelēma will Natural self-determining desire of rational nature. gnōmē / gnomic willing intention, inclination, deliberative mode The personal, historically conditioned mode of willing; in fallen creatures often marked by ignorance, vacillation, and division. tropos mode, manner How a nature is concretely actualized; central for Maximus’s account of personhood and moral life. energeia activity, operation The natural activity through which a nature is actualized.

This vocabulary immediately shows why Maximus cannot be mapped neatly onto modern debates. He is not asking only “what is consciousness?” or “is mind reducible to matter?” He is asking how a rational creature, through embodied sensation, reason, affect, will, and contemplative intellect, moves either toward its logos in God or into fragmentation. His “mind” is therefore teleological from the start.

3. The human being: neither Cartesian dualism nor materialism

Maximus’s anthropology is strongly anti-reductionist. He rejects any view that makes the soul a mere bodily function or denies its continuing activity after bodily death. At the same time, he rejects the idea that the human being is essentially a soul merely using a disposable body. In Ambiguum 7, he treats the human being as a complete composite: body with organs and soul with intellect. The soul is simple and incorporeal, yet it permeates the whole body, gives life and motion to it, and is present to the body’s members according to their capacities.

This is not Cartesian dualism. The soul is not a sealed-off inner subject looking out through a bodily machine. Nor is the body an accidental prison. Soul and body are parts of the one human form; Maximus insists against both pre-existent soul theories and delayed-animation theories that body and soul come into being together as the parts of one human being. Even after death, the soul remains the soul of a particular human being, retaining relational reference to the whole human form rather than becoming a generic immaterial substance.

This gives a first major philosophical conclusion: the Maximian mind is embodied without being materialistically reducible. Mental life belongs to an incorporeal rational soul, but the soul’s life is naturally ordered to bodily expression. The body is not merely the external container of mind; it is the soul’s proper partner in human nature. A modern label such as “hylomorphic dualism” comes closer than Cartesianism or reductive physicalism, but even that must be modified: for Maximus, the form-matter relation is embedded in a cosmic and Christological account of creation, deification, and the resurrection.

Recent scholarship has rightly emphasized this wholeness. Oleksandr Kashchuk’s study describes Maximus’s human being as a dynamic bodily-spiritual individual whose aspiration toward God includes mind, reason, spirit, will, sense, passionate powers, and body. Michael Bakker similarly notes that Maximian psychology cannot be separated from epistemology and anthropology, and that sense perception functions as a border between body and soul rather than as an evil intrusion.

4. The soul’s architecture: intellect, reason, sense, desire, and incensive power

Maximus’s most important architectural distinction is between the contemplative and active powers of the rational soul. In the Mystagogy, he correlates the soul, body, Church, and cosmos. The Church is like a human being: sanctuary as soul, altar as mind, nave as body. Conversely, the human being is a “mystical church.” In that symbolic anthropology, bodily moral wisdom, psychic natural contemplation, and noetic mystical theology are not separate compartments but ascending modes of one integrated creature.

The Mystagogy also gives a crucial faculty schema. The soul has an intellectual and vital dimension; the contemplative power is called mind or intellect, while the active power is called reason. When directed to God, mind becomes wisdom; reason becomes prudence when it orders life rightly. Maximus links these pairs: mind/reason, wisdom/prudence, contemplation/action, knowledge/virtue, enduring knowledge/faith, truth/goodness. This is one of the clearest places to extract a philosophy of mind: theoretical cognition and practical agency are distinct but inseparable.

Bakker’s analysis of Ambiguum 10 reinforces this. Maximus coordinates intellect, reason, and sense with the soul’s motions and with the stages of being, well-being, and eternal being. Intellect is supreme, but reason has the indispensable role of ordering thought and practice. Sense perception is natural and good; it mediates the visible world and gives the soul material for contemplation. The hierarchy is not a rejection of lower powers, but their ordering.

The passible powers also belong to the structure of mind. Desire and the incensive power are not foreign intrusions. In disordered form they become lust, greed, resentment, aggression, fear, or vainglory; in purified form desire becomes divine eros and the incensive power becomes loving zeal. Bronwen Neil’s work on Maximus’s spiritual psychology is especially important here: Maximus does not simply repeat a pessimistic Evagrian suspicion of the passions, but develops a more constructive account in which the passions can become instruments of holy love.

Thus a Maximian map of mental life looks something like this: sense gives contact with the world; memory and imagination preserve and recombine impressions; reason discerns, compares, judges, and directs action; desire and incensive power energize movement; noûs contemplates truth and God; will moves the whole person toward or away from the good; love integrates the entire system. Pathology occurs when lower powers usurp, when images are fused with passions, when reason serves self-love, or when the intellect is distracted from God.

5. Intellect as contemplative awareness, not mere calculation

Noûs is the key term. It is often translated “intellect,” but this can mislead if “intellect” is heard as abstract reasoning. In Maximus, noûs is the highest contemplative power of the soul. It is capable of direct awareness of intelligible reality, contemplation of the logoi of creatures, pure prayer, and, by grace, participation in divine illumination. It is not the same as discursive reason, though it is not irrational. It is more like spiritual-intellectual perception.

In the Four Hundred Texts on Love, Maximus describes the purified intellect as receiving divine light, contemplating visible and invisible things, and entering prayer beyond ordinary conceptual images. He distinguishes between the intellect occupied with passion-free images of human affairs, the intellect engaged in natural contemplation of visible or invisible things, and the intellect illumined by the light of the Holy Trinity.

This yields a rich theory of attention. Attention is not simply a neutral cognitive orientation. It is spiritually and morally charged. The intellect can be tied to material things by passions, darkened by anger and desire, scattered by logismoi, or freed for contemplation. Prayer is not merely a propositional act; it is an attentional purification in which the intellect is cleared of even passion-free images so that it may be present to God.

The intellect also has a peculiar self-transcending capacity. In pure prayer, Maximus can describe the intellect as going beyond created beings and even beyond self-awareness in the ordinary sense. This is not annihilation of mind, but a mode of participation in which the intellect is activated by divine light rather than by its own discursive operations. In modern terms, Maximus has a place for non-discursive, non-representational contemplative awareness. But he would not treat that as a merely private altered state; it is the intellect’s participation in its proper end.

6. Reason as discursive, practical, and therapeutic

Reason, logos in the soul, is not the enemy of contemplation. It is the active and ordering power that helps the soul move rightly. If noûs is contemplative, reason is practical, analytical, and providential in relation to the soul’s vital powers. Maximus’s scheme does not support a simplistic opposition between “mysticism” and “reason.” Rather, reason is necessary for moral practice, discernment of thoughts, the ordering of passions, and the ascent from sensible data to intelligible principles.

This has direct philosophy-of-mind significance. Reason mediates between sensory presentation, conceptual image, affective response, and volitional movement. It can examine thoughts, distinguish their sources, and separate a passion from the representation to which it has attached itself. Bakker explicitly compares aspects of Maximus’s thought-discipline to cognitive therapy: the task is to notice thoughts, examine their causes, distinguish reality from passion-laden interpretation, and reform habits through disciplined practice.

Maximus is therefore neither a pure intellectualist nor an anti-intellectual mystic. Reason is indispensable, but it is not ultimate. It is healed when it serves contemplation, virtue, and love. It becomes distorted when it rationalizes self-love or remains trapped among images, opinions, and apparent goods.

7. Sense perception, body, and embodied cognition

A major mistake would be to read Maximus as if he despised the senses. He repeatedly warns against passion, sensual pleasure, and bodily misuse, but he does not identify body or sensation with evil. In the Four Hundred Texts on Love, he states that the intellect’s natural capacity to form conceptual images, the things perceived, and the senses themselves are not evil, because all are works of God. Evil arises when passion enters into conceptual images and when the mind misuses those images and the things they represent.

This is an important proto-phenomenological distinction: object, mental image, and affective attachment are not identical. The thing is outside the intellect; the conceptual image is formed within it; passion is a disordered affective relation to the thing through the image. The mind’s task is not to destroy images, reject things, or hate the body. It is to separate passion from image, so that the thing can be perceived and used according to its logos.

Maximus’s account of sources of thought is also embodied. The intellect receives impassioned images through the senses, through the body’s condition, and through memory. Bodily states, diet, illness, sleep, and temperament can influence thoughts. He even allows that angels and demons can affect the body’s temperament and thereby alter the thoughts that arise in the intellect. This is not modern neuroscience, but it is a sophisticated account of mind as porous to bodily, mnemonic, sensory, and noetic influences.

The result is not body-denial but disciplined embodiment. The body can become a source of virtue when ordered by the soul, just as the soul is healed when it no longer submits to disordered bodily pleasure. Kashchuk’s summary captures the point well: for Maximus, dynamic aspiration toward God does not require discarding bodily, sensory, or passionate elements, but the proper use and deification of all natural powers.

8. Mental representation: things, images, passions, and thoughts

Maximus’s distinction between things, conceptual images, and passions is one of his most philosophically fertile contributions. A “thing” such as a person, gold, food, or an injury exists outside the mind. A “conceptual image” or thought of that thing exists within the intellect. A “passion” is not the thing and not the image, but a mindless affection, indiscriminate hatred, or disordered attachment linked to the image. An “impassioned thought” is therefore a composite: conceptual content plus passion.

This gives Maximus a powerful account of temptation and mental pathology. We do not sin simply because there are objects in the world or because the mind forms images. We sin when images are used wrongly, when passion adheres to them, and when the will consents to that passion-driven construal. In modern language, Maximus distinguishes perception, representation, affective valence, attentional fixation, and volitional endorsement.

This also explains his ascetic strategy. The monk, and by extension any serious practitioner of mental purification, does not fight against things or even against images as such. The battle is against the passions bound to images. Love and self-control separate the intellect from passions; contemplation separates it from even passion-free images when higher prayer requires it; pure prayer brings the intellect before God.

The same theory allows Maximus to explain intrusive and dream thoughts without reducing them to moral guilt. Some thoughts are simple; others are composite. A passion-charged thought may produce further simple thoughts. Memory, bodily condition, sense impressions, demonic provocation, and habit can all generate mental material. The decisive moral issue is not the mere occurrence of a thought, but the intellect’s relation to it and the will’s use of it.

9. Passions: pathology, neutrality, and transformation

Maximus defines passion in several ways, but the key is motion contrary to nature. Passion is not mere feeling. It is the deformation of a natural power through wrong use, disordered attachment, or movement away from the good. Still, Maximus is more nuanced than a purely suppressive ascetic psychology. Bodily and psychic movements can be natural; the problem is their irrational, self-loving, or anti-natural orientation.

Bronwen Neil’s study is especially useful because it highlights Maximus’s originality. The passions are rooted in earlier Neoplatonic and Evagrian frameworks, but Maximus gives them a more incarnational and communal role. The passible powers are not simply erased; they can be redirected. Desire can become eros for God; the incensive power can become agapic zeal. Apatheia, then, is not emotional numbness. It is the state in which desire and incensive power no longer react irrationally to provocations from things or images.

Maximus also gives a genealogy of disordered mental life through philautia, self-love. Self-love is not healthy self-respect; it is mindless bodily self-attachment. From it arise the principal thoughts of gluttony, avarice, vainglory, and the wider family of passions. This becomes a pleasure-pain cycle: the person seeks pleasure, fears pain, and becomes more enslaved to both. Such pathology is psychological, moral, and ontological at once because it misdirects the creature’s movement from being toward well-being.

The therapeutic answer is not repression but reordering. Fasting, vigils, prayer, mercy, compassion, spiritual reading, watchfulness, and love are not arbitrary religious exercises. They are psychotechnologies of reorientation. They change attention, weaken compulsive associations, retrain desire, calm anger, and free the intellect for truthful perception. Maximus’s psychology is therefore virtue-theoretic: stable mental health is not merely the absence of symptoms, but the acquired condition in which the soul’s powers function according to nature and beyond nature by grace.

10. Will, agency, and freedom

Maximus’s doctrine of will is among his most important contributions to Christian thought, and it is central to any philosophy of mind extracted from him. In his anti-Monothelite writings, he distinguishes natural will from gnomic willing. Natural will is the rational creature’s natural power of desire for what accords with its nature. It is not an optional add-on; a rational nature without natural will would be incomplete.

Andrew Louth’s exposition of Maximus is useful here: willing is rooted in rational nature as a natural desire corresponding to that nature. Human freedom is self-determination, but not directionless arbitrariness. Created freedom is ordered toward God, because the human being’s nature finds fulfillment in freely turning toward the God who gives it being.

Gnomic willing is more complex. In fallen creatures, we often do not clearly know the good. We deliberate, hesitate, compare apparent goods, and act from opinion or inclination. Maximus calls this gnomic willing. It is the historically conditioned, personal mode in which the natural will is exercised under conditions of partial knowledge and division. Bradshaw’s analysis stresses that gnomic willing is not a separate faculty alongside natural will; it is a mode or act of willing made possible by the natural will.

This is philosophically significant because Maximus separates freedom from sheer alternativity. The highest freedom is not the ability to choose evil. That is a sign of ignorance and instability, not perfection. Christ has a natural human will, because he has a complete human nature, but Maximus denies that Christ has a gnomic will in the fallen deliberative sense. Christ’s human will is not double-minded, ignorant of the good, or divided from the divine will.

In modern terms, Maximus rejects both determinism and voluntarist arbitrariness. The will is self-determining, but it is teleologically structured. Reason informs choice but does not mechanically determine it. Perfect freedom is lucid movement toward the good; fallen freedom is the unstable exercise of natural desire through partial knowledge, self-love, and divided inclination.

11. The sequence of action: from thought to use

Maximus also gives a sequential account of willing and action. Bradshaw summarizes a Maximian sequence: we think, wish, search, consider, deliberate, judge, incline, choose, move, and use. This is not a modern action theory, but it is recognizably a theory of practical cognition. It places action at the end of a chain involving representation, desire, inquiry, deliberation, judgment, inclination, and bodily use.

The final category, “use,” is crucial. For Maximus, created things are good, but their moral significance depends on use. The same is true of thoughts. Evil does not have its own positive substance; it appears through deprivation, misjudgment, irrational motion, and wrong use of natural powers. This is why the mind’s relation to images is so important. A thought is not evil simply by existing; evil arises when the thought is used contrary to nature and the good.

This account has a strong philosophy-of-mind implication: cognition is not complete until one asks how mental content is used. A representation is not merely a representation; it is embedded in a practical and affective economy. The same image can be passion-free, desire-laden, hateful, reverent, contemplative, or prayerfully transcended. Mental content is therefore not fully intelligible apart from the agent’s orientation.

12. Selfhood, personhood, and the soul

Maximus does not simply identify the self with the mind. The human person is not “the intellect” alone, nor “the soul” alone, nor “the body” alone. Personhood is bound to hypostasis, concrete mode of existence, and the way a nature is lived. Louth captures this well: what is unique about each of us is what we have made of the nature we have—our mode of existence, our past, hopes, and concrete life of nature.

Aleksandar Djakovac’s study of the soul in Maximus identifies a tension between two lines of thought: one in which the soul is treated as an incorporeal, self-subsisting entity in a Neoplatonic register, and another shaped by Cappadocian and Christological concerns with person and hypostasis. This tension is not merely a defect. It reveals why Maximus cannot be reduced either to a Platonic soul-metaphysics or to a purely relational account of personhood.

The best synthesis is this: the soul is an incorporeal, rational, life-bearing part of the human being, capable of activity beyond bodily death; but the soul is not the complete human person in abstraction from body, history, relation, and mode of existence. The person is the concrete “who” who lives a nature through body, soul, intellect, will, passions, and relation to God and others. This is why Maximus’s philosophy of mind is also a philosophy of moral selfhood.

13. Knowledge, logoi, and natural contemplation

Maximus’s theory of mind cannot be separated from his theory of intelligibility. Created beings are intelligible because they have logoi in the Logos. The mind does not impose intelligibility on a meaningless world; it discovers, contemplates, and participates in the intelligible principles by which creatures exist. In Ambiguum 7, Maximus describes the contemplative soul discerning the logos according to which things were created and thereby knowing the one Logos as many logoi and the many logoi as one Logos.

This is not a representationalist theory in the modern secular sense. The logoi are not merely concepts in the human mind. Nor are they mental pictures in God’s mind. They are the divine-rational principles, intentions, or wills through which created beings have their being and order. A Russian-language study of Ambiguum 41, following the Dionysian background, explicitly identifies the logoi of beings as divine and good predefinitions or wishes according to which the superessential God predetermines and brings beings into existence.

Natural contemplation is therefore a disciplined cognition of creation as meaningful. Sense perception provides contact with visible things; reason analyzes and orders; intellect contemplates the logoi; love prevents knowledge from becoming domination. A purely detached or exploitative cognition would not be true knowledge in the Maximian sense, because it would fail to perceive beings according to their divine purpose.

14. God and the “mind of God”

The divine mind must be treated carefully. Maximus is not saying that God is simply the largest mind, a cosmic intellect, or a disembodied subject with thoughts. In the Two Hundred Texts on Theology and the Incarnate Dispensation, he explicitly warns that God is not to be conceived as intellect or intelligible being; God is beyond intellection and intelligibility. This apophatic boundary is non-negotiable.

Yet Maximus does not therefore exclude God from a philosophy of mind. Quite the opposite: God is the condition of created mind’s intelligibility, end, and transformation. Created intellect can know God only through participation, contemplation of creation, providence, and finally unknowing. The mind’s highest act is not possession of God as an object, but being drawn beyond finite intellection into deifying communion.

The Logos/logoi doctrine is the key. God does not “think” creatures by receiving information from them, as human minds do. Rather, creatures exist according to logoi in the Logos. Divine knowing is causal, creative, and providential, not receptive or discursive. The “mind of God,” if one uses that phrase, must therefore be understood analogically and apophatically: God is the source of all intelligibility, but not a member of the class “intelligent beings.”

This has a profound consequence for Maximian philosophy of mind. Human mind is not autonomous reason standing over against a neutral world. It is a created participation in intelligibility whose final fulfillment is beyond conceptual mastery. The highest knowledge is not irrational, but it is trans-rational: “unknowing” is not ignorance, but the mind’s reverent encounter with what exceeds created concepts.

15. Angelic minds and the noetic ecology

Angels are not theological decorations in Maximus. They are part of the noetic structure of reality. Maximus speaks of rational and intellective beings, including angels and humans, as created according to logoi in God. In Ambiguum 7, he imagines the final state of rational creation—angels and human beings—filled with spiritual joy as the intellect is illumined by the Trinity.

The Mystagogy also presents liturgical ascent as a joining with the incorporeal intelligent powers. Human beings, through the Church’s liturgy, are brought into a mode of praise and union that aligns them with angelic intelligences while not erasing the distinctively human union of body and soul, sensible and intelligible worlds.

Angels matter philosophically because they show that intellective life is not identical with human embodied cognition. There are incorporeal intelligences. But Maximus does not conclude that the human ideal is to stop being bodily and become angelic. Humanity has its own cosmic role: the human being mediates sensible and intelligible creation. The resurrection does not abolish body; it transfigures it.

Demons are the negative side of the same noetic ecology. Maximus does not regard demons as evil by nature; they became evil through misuse of natural powers. They can provoke, intensify, or manipulate thoughts, especially where passions already provide an opening. A modern reader need not flatten this into mere metaphor to see the philosophical point: for Maximus, mental life is not sealed inside an isolated subject. It is exposed to bodily states, memories, social relations, angelic help, demonic provocation, divine grace, and liturgical formation.

16. The mind as liturgical

The Mystagogy is indispensable for extracting Maximus’s philosophy of mind because it prevents us from construing mind as merely private. The mind is formed liturgically. The Church is a symbolic and operative structure in which the soul’s faculties are purified, ordered, and elevated. The nave, sanctuary, and altar correspond to body, soul, and mind; liturgical movement corresponds to psychic ascent; the Eucharistic gathering images the integration of the human being and cosmos.

This is not merely allegory. For Maximus, symbol participates in reality. The liturgy trains perception. It closes the doors of the senses to passionate distraction, leads the soul from sensible forms to intelligible principles, unites the soul’s powers, and brings the faithful into communion with angelic praise and divine life.

A Maximian philosophy of mind is therefore also a philosophy of attention-formation. The mind is shaped by repeated practices, images, gestures, words, loves, and communal order. There is no purely “unformed” mind. One is formed either by passions and self-love or by liturgy, virtue, contemplation, and love.

17. The social and communal dimension of mind

Maximus is often read as an ascetic solitary, but his psychology is not individualistic. Neil emphasizes that Maximus’s treatment of the passions has a communal dimension: passions are relational, and healing requires humility, love, and often guidance from another. In the Four Hundred Texts, Maximus warns that the foolish intellect clings to its own thoughts as if they were beautiful children; the wise person distrusts even persuasive thoughts and submits them to the judgment of the wise.

This is a striking account of epistemic humility. Mental distortion is not only a matter of false propositions but of possessive attachment to one’s own cognition. The mind loves its own thoughts. That love can be pathological. Correction requires community, obedience, and the willingness to let one’s inner representations be judged by another.

In modern terms, Maximus has a socially extended therapy of mind. The mind is not healed by introspection alone. It is healed by practices, teachers, liturgy, community, and love of enemies. This is one reason his philosophy of mind cannot be reduced to an individualist account of consciousness.

18. A systematic extraction: twelve theses of a Maximian philosophy of mind

The following theses condense the report into a philosophical model.

First, mind is a power of an embodied rational soul, not a function of matter alone. The soul is incorporeal and irreducible, but the complete human being is body-soul unity. Human mind is therefore embodied without being materialistically reducible.

Second, the human being is not identical with the mind. Personhood is the concrete hypostatic mode of living a nature through body, soul, intellect, reason, will, affect, history, and relation. The noûs is highest in the soul, but it is not the whole person.

Third, cognition is teleological. Mental powers are defined by their natural ends: truth, goodness, God, and the logoi of beings. A cognitive act is not fully understood by its internal content alone, but by the movement of the person in which it participates.

Fourth, cognition is hierarchical but integrated. Sense, memory, imagination, reason, and intellect are ordered powers. Lower powers are not evil; they become pathological when detached from their proper order.

Fifth, mental representation is morally and affectively modifiable. A thing, an image of a thing, and a passion attached to that image are distinct. This distinction is central to Maximus’s account of temptation, perception, and therapy.

Sixth, passions are not mere emotions but disordered movements of natural powers. Their cure is not extinction of affect but transformation into love, divine desire, courage, mercy, and stable dispassion.

Seventh, attention is spiritual agency. The intellect can cling, scatter, darken, purify, contemplate, or pray. What one attends to, and how one attends, is constitutive of mental and moral life.

Eighth, freedom is teleological self-determination, not arbitrary choice. The natural will desires the good according to nature; gnomic willing is the personal mode of willing under historical and often fallen conditions. Perfect freedom is lucid movement toward the good, not oscillation between good and evil.

Ninth, Christology clarifies anthropology. Christ’s complete human mind, soul, body, activity, and will show what unfallen human agency is: not less human because free from gnomic vacillation, but more perfectly human.

Tenth, God is beyond mind but is the source of mind. God cannot be treated as one intellect among others, yet the divine Logos grounds the intelligibility of all beings and the possibility of created intellect.

Eleventh, angelic minds and demonic minds belong to the full noetic ecology. Human mental life is not sealed off from supra-human created intelligences, though the human vocation remains distinctively body-soul and cosmic.

Twelfth, the final state of mind is deification, not mere information. The intellect’s perfection is not exhaustive conceptual control, but union, participation, and ever-deepening reception of divine light beyond ordinary knowledge.

19. Relation to modern philosophy of mind

Maximus is not a reductive materialist. He rejects the reduction of rational activity to bodily effects and insists on the incorporeal soul. But he is also not a Cartesian dualist, because mind is not an isolated thinking substance externally attached to a machine-body. His view is closer to a participatory hylomorphic anthropology: the soul is the life and rational principle of the body, the body is the proper expression and partner of the soul, and the human person is the concrete unity of both.

He is also close, in some respects, to embodied cognition. Bodily condition, sense perception, diet, memory, sleep, and habit affect thoughts. The senses are not evil; they are natural channels of cognition. But Maximus goes beyond most modern embodied cognition by placing embodiment within cosmic mediation and deification.

His account of conceptual images and passions resembles affective intentionalism. Emotions and passions are not raw inner feelings; they are directed toward objects as construed through images, judgments, and desires. A passion changes the way an object appears to the mind. Healing therefore requires changing not only behavior but perception, attention, and affective construal.

His account of watchfulness resembles cognitive therapy, but with deeper metaphysical and ascetic commitments. Thoughts are noticed, classified, tested, and deprived of their compulsive force; the causes of thoughts are investigated; habits are retrained. Yet the goal is not merely psychological equilibrium, but purity of heart, love, contemplation, and union with God.

His philosophy of agency resists modern assumptions that freedom is primarily choice among alternatives. For Maximus, deliberation often signals lack of clear vision. The freer the mind becomes, the more spontaneously and wholly it moves toward the true good. This places Maximus closer to virtue ethics than to voluntarist choice theory: the perfected agent does not become less free by becoming unable to desire evil; he becomes free precisely because the will is no longer divided.

20. Non-English reception and interpretive signals

French, Russian, and East European materials confirm that an adequate philosophy-of-mind reading should not be limited to Anglophone debates about will. An early French study by M.-Th. Disdier frames Maximian spirituality around original image and likeness, deformation by sin, and restoration through active and contemplative life. It explicitly notes that Maximus’s spirituality cannot be understood without the major lines of his psychology and observes his rejection of both Platonic/Origenist pre-existence and delayed ensoulment.

Russian-language reception often foregrounds hierarchy, noûs, and ascetic anthropology. One Russian article on hierarchical anthropology emphasizes that the human mind is called to communion with God, is meant to govern the motions of the flesh, is mutable when turned toward created things, and is preserved especially by love. Another Russian study of Ambiguum 41 stresses that “mind” and “reason” should not be treated as two separate organs of the soul, but as contemplative and active capacities of the one rational power.

East European scholarship also emphasizes wholeness. Kashchuk’s article, published in a Polish patristic journal by a Ukrainian scholar, states that Maximus treats the human being as a whole: not only mind, reason, and spirit, but also will, sense, passionate powers, and body. This is a helpful corrective to overly intellectualist readings.

The Serbian scholar Aleksandar Djakovac adds another important interpretive caution: Maximus’s doctrine of the soul contains both a Neoplatonic tendency to treat the soul as incorporeal entity and a Cappadocian-Christological tendency to read human truth through personhood and hypostatic relation. A rigorous philosophy of mind should preserve this tension rather than prematurely resolving it.

21. Open interpretive problems

Several issues remain genuinely difficult.

The first is the precise relation between noûs and logos. Some texts distinguish them sharply as contemplative and active powers; others allow more fluidity. It is safest to say that they are distinct functions within the rational soul rather than separate substances or organs.

The second is whether Maximus has a concept analogous to the unconscious. He does not have a Freudian unconscious, but he clearly recognizes thoughts arising from memory, bodily condition, sleep, demonic provocation, and habit before reflective endorsement. That is enough to say he has a layered theory of mental emergence, but not a modern depth psychology.

The third is the status of gnomic will. In Christological polemic, Maximus denies gnomic will in Christ because gnomic deliberation implies ignorance or division. In broader anthropology, gnōmē can name the concrete personal mode of willing. The safest interpretation is that natural will is the faculty rooted in nature, while gnomic willing is the personal-historical mode in which the faculty is exercised, often but not necessarily pathologically.

The fourth is whether the soul is an entity or a relational principle of personhood. Maximus says enough to support the soul’s incorporeality and post-mortem activity, but he also refuses to make the soul the whole human person. Djakovac’s “entity or person” tension is therefore real and productive.

The fifth is how literally to read angelic and demonic influence on thought. A historically faithful reading must not dismiss it as mere metaphor. At the philosophical level, it means that Maximus’s theory of mind is not closed-subject internalism. Minds exist in a noetic field involving other created intelligences, grace, temptation, and liturgy.

The sixth is how to speak of divine mind. Maximus’s apophaticism forbids treating God as a supreme instance of creaturely intellect, but his Logos/logoi doctrine forbids excluding God from the theory of intelligibility. God is beyond mind as its transcendent source, not below mind as an unknowable blank.

22. Final synthesis

A comprehensive Maximian philosophy of mind can be stated as follows.

Mind is the highest contemplative activity of a rational, incorporeal soul naturally united to a body in a concrete human person. It receives the world through sense, forms images, reasons discursively, desires, becomes angry, remembers, imagines, wills, chooses, acts, and prays. Its proper function is not merely to represent the world, but to perceive beings according to their logoi, use them rightly, love them without passion, and move through them toward God.

Mental disorder is not primarily lack of information. It is disordered movement: passion fused with image, desire fixed on pleasure, anger fixed on injury, reason serving self-love, intellect distracted from God, will divided by ignorance and gnomic instability. Mental healing is therefore not mere cognition but purification, re-habituation, truthful perception, rightly ordered love, and deification.

God is not one mind among minds, but the transcendent source of all intelligibility. Angels are real intellective creatures whose existence expands the field of mind beyond human embodiment, while demons show the possibility of noetic distortion without ontological evil in nature. Human beings are not angels trapped in bodies; they are body-soul microcosms called to unite the sensible and intelligible creation in Christ.

The final Maximian answer to “what is mind?” is therefore: mind is created intellect in motion toward its divine archetype, embodied in a human person, trained by reason and virtue, purified from passionate images, illumined by the logoi of beings, integrated by love, and fulfilled by participation in the unknowable God.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
